coredata.dc:title: Tumor cell-on fluorescence imaging agent using hyaluronate dots
coredata.dc:creator: Choi, Eun Jung
Park, Hongsuk
Noh, Gwang Jin
Lee, Eun Seong
coredata.dc:description: We report here the use of near-infrared fluorescent dye-labelled hyaluronate (HA) dot named HDFc for tumor imaging. We took advantage of the unique auto-quenching characteristic that occurs when the fluorescent dye molecules are in close proximity to one another under ordinary conditions. However, when the HDFc is located in tumor cells, the tumor cell-specific enzyme (e.g., hyaluronidase: HAase) affects the structure of the HDFc, followed by the transition from auto-quenched dye molecules to dequenched dye molecules, resulting in the identification of the tumor cells. For this purpose, HDFcs were synthesized, characterized, and exogenously treated with HAase to demonstrate the enzyme-dependent HDFc photoactivity. Specifically, confocal microscopy and flow cytometry confirmed the efficient cellular internalization and fluorescence production of HDFc in CD44+ and HAase-abundant tumor cells. Collectively, this study opens the door for utilizing polymeric dots to visualize tumor cells by introducing biocompatible HA and tumor cell-on photoluminescent dye.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hyaluronate dots
Tumor cell imaging
Hyaluronidase
CD44 receptor-medicated endocytosis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and fluorescence intensity of HDFcs
Synthesis and characterization of HDFc particles

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of tumor cell-on HDFc. (b) Synthesis scheme of the HDFc. (c) TEM images of the HA dot (HD) and HDFc32. (d) Zeta potential of HD, HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32 (0.1 mg/mL, pH 7.4, 150 mM) (n = 3) (* p < 0.01 compared to HD). (e) Particle size distribution of the HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32 without HAase (left panel) or with HAase (right panel) at pH 7.4.

(a) NIR fluorescence images of HD (0.1 mg/mL), Fc (10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), and HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) after incubation with different concentrations of HAase (0–1000 U/mL) for 60 min. (b) NIR fluorescence images of HD (0.1 mg/mL), Fc (10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), and HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) after incubation with 1000 U/mL of HAase during different time courses (0–60 min). (c) Emission spectra (at λex 400 nm and λem 600–750 nm) of HD (0.1 mg/mL), Fc (10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), and HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) after incubation with HAase (1000 U/mL) for 60 min. (d) Dequenching efficiencies of Fc (10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), and HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) after incubation under different concentrations of HAase (0–1000 U/mL). [Dequenching efficiency = Fluorescence intensity of each sample/Fluorescence intensity of Fc] (n = 3) (* p < 0.01 compared to Fc) (e) Relative quantum yields of Fc (10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), and HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) after incubation under different concentrations of HAase (0–1000 U/mL). [Relative quantum yield (Φx) = ΦST (Gradx/GradST) (ηx 2/ηST 2)] (n = 3) (* p < 0.01 compared to Fc).

(a) NIR fluorescence images from different types of cells after incubation with the Fc (10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), or HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) for 8 h. (b) Relative fluorescence intensities on different types of cells after incubation with Fc (10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), or HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) for 8 h (* p < 0.01 compared to NIH3T3). (c) Western blot analysis of Hyal-1 levels and densitometric quantifications in each cell line using ImageJ software (lower) (n = 3) (* p < 0.01 compared to NIH3T3 cells). Confocal images and flow cytometry of (d–e) MDA-MB-231 or (f–g) NIH3T3 cells after treatment with Fc (10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), or HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) at 37℃ for 8 h.

Confocal time-lapse fluorescence analysis and relative fluorescence intensities of (a–b) MDA-MB-231 or (c–d) NIH3T3 cells after treatment with Fc (10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), or HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) for 120 min. In each panel, the cell nuclei stained with Hoechst 33342 are blue, the cell membranes stained with WGA-Alexa Fluor ® 488 are green, and the Ce6 dischared fluorescence signals are red. The white arrows indicate the intracellular localization of HDFc particles. Fluorescence intensities were measured from the confocal images using ImageJ software (n = 3) (* p < 0.01 compared to Fc). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Tumor cell-on fluorescence imaging agent using hyaluronate dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, The Catholic University of Korea, 43 Jibong-ro, Bucheon-si, Gyeonggi-do, 14662, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Endocrinology, Metabolism & Lipid Research, Department of Medicine, Washington University School of Medicine, Saint Louis, MO, 63110, USA

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report here the use of near-infrared fluorescent dye-labelled hyaluronate (HA) dot named HDFc for tumor imaging. We took advantage of the unique auto-quenching characteristic that occurs when the fluorescent dye molecules are in close proximity to one another under ordinary conditions. However, when the HDFc is located in tumor cells, the tumor cell-specific enzyme (e.g., hyaluronidase: HAase) affects the structure of the HDFc, followed by the transition from auto-quenched dye molecules to dequenched dye molecules, resulting in the identification of the tumor cells. For this purpose, HDFcs were synthesized, characterized, and exogenously treated with HAase to demonstrate the enzyme-dependent HDFc photoactivity. Specifically, confocal microscopy and flow cytometry confirmed the efficient cellular internalization and fluorescence production of HDFc in CD44+ and HAase-abundant tumor cells. Collectively, this study opens the door for utilizing polymeric dots to visualize tumor cells by introducing biocompatible HA and tumor cell-on photoluminescent dye.

ce:section.ce:para: For the past several decades, diagnostic imaging technologies have placed many potential tumor patients at a significantly lower risk of disease development. Imaging is not simply photography, but involves utilizing diverse methods to produce a 2-dimensional picture, including computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, and positron emission tomography. In particular, nanoparticle-based imaging has caught the attention of many researchers, because nanoparticles can be readily decorated with additional contrast agents, such as cost-effective, sensitive, and selective fluorescent dyes (Kircher et al., 2012; Pérez-Medina et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015). A variety of imaging nanoparticles have been designed and developed with unique physicochemical characteristics, including shape, size, and/or surface chemistry. Among them, polysaccharide-based or polymer-associated nanoconstructs have demonstrated effective access to tumor tissues as a biomarker (Pu, Chattopadhyay, & Rao, 2016; Saravanakumar, Jo, & Park, 2012; Swierczewska, Han, Kim, Park, & Lee, 2016; Xie, Upputuri, Zhen, Pramanik, & Pu, 2017).

Emerging studies suggest that biodegradable and biocompatible polymeric dots are a promising tool to mitigate the disadvantages of conventional dots, such as non-degradability and toxicity (Choi, Lee, Youn, Na, & Lee, 2018; Jokerst, Lobovkina, Zare, & Gambhir, 2011). Polysaccharide-based ingredients provide not only biofunctionality but also biocompatibility in the preparation of imaging nanoparticles (Li et al., 2016; Wu et al., 2010; Yang, Han, Zheng, Dong, & Liu, 2015). Thus, the polysaccharide-involving polymeric particles are expected to advance the field of biomedical diagnosis. More specifically, as a versatile polysaccharide, hyaluronate (HA) can be introduced into this technology, because it is a naturally occurring linear polysaccharide, serving as a joint lubricant, and has been intensively investigated in bio-cargo delivery applications. Furthermore, HA actively binds to the CD44 receptor, which is overexpressed on certain types of tumor cells, resulting in receptor-medicated endocytosis of HA-anchored imaging nanoparticles into the tumor cells (Choi et al., 2018; Lee, Park, Noh, & Lee, 2018; Lee, Park, Oh, & Lee, 2018; Yang et al., 2012).

Tumor cell-specific intracellular metabolism could be of potential utility to differentiate tumor cells from normal cells, and this feature can be exploited to design and develop a tumor cell-selective imaging probe. In pursuing such a strategy, a substrate degradable by an enzyme that is enriched in tumor cells can be introduced into an imaging vehicle in order to have the tumor cell-responsive activity. In this light, the approach of employing a hyaluronidase (HAase, exclusively abundant enzyme in tumor cells)-HA (substrate of HAase) reaction mechanism would allow for the creation of a tumor cell-sensitive imaging tool (Li et al., 2014; McAtee, Barycki, & Simpson, 2014). HAase degrades glycosidic bonds of HA into diverse oligosaccharides, and there are five isotypes of HAase in humans. Among them, hyaluronidase-1 (Hyal-1) is representative among them, and is expressed in most tissues, contributing to the intracellular hydrolysis of HA (Stern & Jedrzejas, 2006).

Previously, we demonstrated the use of extremely small-sized HA dots for photodynamic tumor therapy (Choi et al., 2018). Beyond functioning as the critical conjugate for efficient cellular entry, it is noteworthy that HA provided in vivo biocompatibility of the dots. Herein, HA dots with different ratios of fluorescent dye (chlorin e6: Ce6) (named HDFcs) were synthesized, characterized, and assessed as a tumor cell-selective and -sensitive monitoring device. It is known that given low-intensity light, Ce6 dye molecules can produce harmless fluorescence in cells (Choi et al., 2018; Lee, Park, Noh et al., 2018). Furthermore, since the fluorescence emission by Ce6 molecules is not turned on as long as they are auto-quenched, the introduction of tumor cell-specialized dequenching into clinical diagnosis will reveal new sources for tumor-targeted bioimaging. To this end, three types of HDFcs (HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32) were prepared to examine their healthy, normal cell-off and diseased, tumor cell-on properties.

Hyaluronic acid (HA, Mn = 3.7 kDa), N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), adipic acid dihydrazide (ADH), triethylamine (TEA), hyaluronidase from bovine testes type 1 (Hyal-1), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), toluene, 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI), and Hoechst 33342 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Chlorin e6 (Ce6) was obtained from Frontier Scientific Inc. (USA). RPMI-1640, Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s media (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin, trypsin, ethylene diamine tetra-acetic acid (EDTA), and streptomycin were purchased from Welgene Inc. (Korea). Bovine serum (BS) was obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (USA). Wheat Germ Agglutinin Alexa Fluor® 488 conjugate (WGA-Alexa Fluor® 488) was purchased from Life Technologies (USA). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) was obtained from Dojindo Molecular Technologies Inc. (Japan). Anti-HYAL1 antibody and goat anti-rabbit IgG H&L secondary antibody were purchased from Abcam (UK).

The HA dot (HD) was synthesized as previously reported (Choi et al., 2018). Briefly, HA and C60 were reacted in DMSO/toluene with LiOH at 25 °C for 3 days, followed by evaporation using a rotary evaporator. After that, the solution was dialyzed against a pre-swollen dialysis membrane tube in DMSO for 2 days and additionally, in deionized water for 2 days to purify it. After lyophilization, the acquired HD (200 mg) was pre-activated in DMSO (20 mL) with DCC (20 mg) and NHS (10 mg) for 1 day. Then, Ce6 (115 mg) with DCC (20 mg), NHS (10 mg), ADH (15 mg), and TEA (1 mL) was additionally added into the DMSO solution and the mixture was reacted at 25 °C for 7 days to produce the HA dot conjugated with fluorescent Ce6 (HDFc32, 32 indicates the average molar ratio of attached Ce6 to HD). For HDFc22 (22 indicates the average molar ratio of linked Ce6 to HD) synthesis, the same ingredients as those used for HDFc32 preparation were reacted at 25 °C for 4 days. Both products were obtained after purifying the solution using a dialysis membrane (Spectra/Por® MWCO 2 kDa) in DMSO for 2 days and in deionized water for 2 days, followed by lyophilization. To prepare HDFc12 (12 indicates the average molar ratio of anchored Ce6 to HD), HD (100 mg) was pre-activated in DMSO (15 mL) with DCC (10 mg) and NHS (5 mg) for 1 day, and Ce6 (70 mg), DCC (10 mg), and NHS (7 mg) were then reacted with the solution for 4 days, followed by dialysis and lyophilization.

The chemical identity of HDFc32, HDFc22, and HDFc12 was determined using a Bruker 300 MHz NMR Spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Sizes and surface charges of HDFc32, HDFc22, and HDFc12 dispersed (without requiring additional sonication procedure) in PBS (pH 7.4, 150 mM) with or without HAase (1000 U/mL) were measured using a Zetasizer 3000 (Malvern Instruments, USA). The morphology of particles was monitored using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM 1010, Japan).

To investigate the degree of dequenching in HDFcs by HAase, HAase (from 0 to 1000 U/mL) was treated with the HD (without Ce6, 100 μg/mL), free Ce6 (Fc, 10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), and HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL). Fluorescence images were visualized during a time course of HAase treatment (0–60 min) using Image Station 4000 MM (Kodak, USA) (Park et al., 2012).

Fluorescence intensity of the HD (100 μg/mL), Fc (10 μg/mL), HDFc12 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), HDFc22 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), and HDFc32 (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) under HAase treatment (0–1000 U/mL) was measured at the wavelengths of λex 400 nm and λem 600–750 nm using a Shimadzu RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Japan). Based on the intensity results, dequenching efficiency (DE) is defined as the ratio of each HDFc’s florescence intensity to Fc’s fluorescence intensity. Integrated fluorescence intensity is determined as a sum of the intensity values over the emission profile. It is calculated by the following equation (Wall, Dillon, & Knowles, 2015): relative quantum yield (Φx) = ΦST (Gradx/ GradST)(ηx 2/ηST 2), where, ΦST (quantum yield of Fc in this study) is 0.16 (Bastien et al., 2015), Gradx is the slope of integrated fluorescence intensity of each HDFcs as a function of absorbance, GradST is the slope of the standard (Fc in this study), and ηx and ηST are reflective indices of HDFc solution and Fc solution, respectively.

Human breast adenocarcinoma MDA-MB-231 and MCF 7 cells, human colorectal carcinoma HCT-116 cells, and human lung carcinoma A549 cells were maintained in RPMI medium supplemented with 10% FBS. Human cervical adenocarcinoma HeLa and mouse embryo fibroblast NIH3T3 cells were maintained in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS. All of the cells were cultured at 37 °C under 5% CO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity and fluorescence intensity of HDFcs
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the cell toxicity of the HDFcs, MDA-MB-231, HCT-116, and NIH3T3 cells were seeded on 96-well plates, and Fc (10 μg/mL), or each HDFC sample (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) was added. After washing the cells, their viability was evaluated using the CCK-8 assay.

To investigate quenching status, MDA-MB-231, MCF-7, HCT-116, A-549, and HeLa cells (2 × 104/well) were monolayer plated for 1 day, and then treated with Fc (10 μg/mL), or each HDFC particle (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) for another 8 h. Fluorescence images were taken using Image Station 4000 MM (Kodak, USA) and the intensity was measured using a Synergy MX fluorescence spectrophotometer (BioTek, USA) (Mok, Jeong, Kim, & Chung, 2012).

Whole-cell lysates were prepared from cells using NETN lysis buffer, containing 20 mM Tris−HCl (pH 8.0), 100 mM NaCl, 1 mM EDTA, and 0.5% Nonidet P-40. Protein (40 μg) was loaded and separated by SDS-PAGE, and transferred to a PVDF membrane (Merck Millipore Ltd, USA). For Hyal-1 immunoblots, membranes were blocked in 5% skim milk in PBS. After that, they were treated with rabbit polyclonal antibodies against Hyal-1 (1:500, Abcam, UK) and mouse monoclonal antibodies against β-actin (1:1000, Santa Cruz, USA) for 1 day. Anti-rabbit heavy and light chain-specific secondary antibodies (1:5000, Abcam, UK) or anti-mouse HRP-conjugated secondary antibodies (1:5000, GeneTex, USA) were then added for 1 h. Proteins were visualized using a chemiluminescence detection system (Amershan Biosciences, USA).

For fixed cell imaging, MDA-MB-231, HCT-116, and NIH3T3 cells were seeded and incubated with Fc (10 μg/mL), or each HDFC particle (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL) for 8 h, followed by staining with Hoechst 33342 and WGA-Alexa Fluor® 488 for the nuclei and cell membrane, respectively. The images were taken using confocal microscopy (CarlZeiss Meta LSM510, Germany).

For live-cell imaging, MDA-MB-231, HCT-116, and NIH3T3 cells were plated on a 35-mm cell culture dish with glass bottom for 1 day. After changing the media to fresh serum (with heat inactivation)-free media containing Fc (10 μg/mL), or each HDFC particle (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL), the cells were stained using Hoechst 33342 and WGA-Alexa Fluor® 488 and incubated for 1 h. Then, the media was changed to fresh serum free media and the live-cell images were monitored during time courses of the particle treatment using confocal microscopy. Relative fluorescence intensity of Ce6 was quantitatively determined using ImageJ software by comparing red Ce6 fluorescence with the green WGA-Alexa Fluor® 488 signal.

For flow cytometry, MDA-MB-231, HCT-116, and NIH3T3 cells were treated with Fc (10 μg/mL), or each HDFC sample (equivalent to Fc 10 μg/mL). Each particle was treated during different time courses of 1, 2, 4, and 8 h. Cellular internalization of particles was measured using a FACSCalibur™ Flow Cytometer (Becton Dickinson, USA).
The results were analyzed using the statistical software program. Statistical significance is represented as p < 0.01 (*) by Student’s t-test or ANOVA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of HDFc particles
ce:section.ce:para: Our proposed imaging nanoparticles (HDFcs) were designed to recognize CD44 receptors on the surface of tumor cells, followed by internalization via receptor-mediated endocytosis, leading to activated Ce6 by the HAase-regulated HDFc destabilization (Fig. 1 a). Because the Ce6 molecules in HDFc is aggregated and auto-quenched, their fluorescence activity is off under normal conditions. However, after cellular uptake into tumor cells, the endogenous HAase interacts with HA and degrades it, and this reaction enables the tight structure of the HDFc to disintegrate, resulting in the Ce6 being turned on (Lim, Park, Lee, & Lee, 2018; Verhille et al., 2010).

The HD was prepared by conjugating HA with C60, and the HD (Mn = 16.3 kDa) is empty inside with 36 carboxyl groups per dot on the surface (Choi et al., 2018). As a conditionally functioning fluorescent dye, Ce6 was attached to the HD using DCC and NHS, resulting in the HDFc (Fig. 1b). The HDFc was synthesized by a simple chemical conjugation unlike the traditional hydrothermal process with high heat (Zhang et al., 2017). Three types of HDFcs, HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32 were made and the subscripts of 12, 22, and 32 indicate the average molar conjugate ratios of Ce6 to HD. The ratios were estimated using the 1H NMR-based integration ratio of peaks at δ 1.23 ppm (CH from HD) and δ 6.16 ppm (CH2 from Ce6) (Fig. S1).

Spherical dot formation of the HD and HDFc32 was confirmed by TEM (Fig. 1c), and the size was approximately 2 nm in diameter. The zeta potential results demonstrated that the HD, HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32 have negative charges due to the existence of HA and Ce6 on the surface (Huang et al., 2011; Liu, Zhao, Cheng, & Liu, 2015). In addition, the negative value was increased in an anchored Ce6 amount-dependent manner (Fig. 1d).

Consistent with the TEM results (Fig. 1c), the hydrodynamic sizes of HDFcs were less than 10 nm under the normal condition without HAase. However, their sizes were dramatically changed to more than 35 nm after HAase treatment, mainly due to the cleavage of HA chain by HAase-elicited hydrolysis and the disintegration of HA chains resulted in the exposure of hydrophobic C60 surface, leading to hydrophobic interaction-based particle aggregation. This dot aggregation increased the particle size. (Fig. 1e). Together, the results suggest that HAase regulates the structure of synthesized HDFcs.

To investigate the photochemical characteristics of the HDFcs, optical images under near-infrared (NIR) laser irradiation were visualized with different concentrations of HAase (Fig. 2 a), and different HAase treatment times (Fig. 2b). Since HD has no fluorescent dye Ce6, it showed no signal regardless of HAase concentration and treatment time. Moreover, HAase did not affect the photoactivity of Fc particles due to the deficiency of functional additives. However, HDFc22 and HDFc32 displayed remarkably increased fluorescence at the highest HAase dose of 1000 U/mL, and at the longest incubation time of 60 min. Similarly, HDFc12 also exhibited HAase concentration- and interaction time-dependent fluorescence elevation. The results indicate that more effective dequenching and activation of Ce6 are highly involved in the concentration of treated HAase and the incubation time of HAase. Interestingly, HDFc22 had a detectable red signal, different from the negligible fluorescence of HDFc12 and HDFc32 without HAase (Fig. 2a). Since HD has 36 carboxyl groups as the linker to Ce6 (Choi et al., 2018), densely coated Ce6 on HDFc32 (32 linkages formed out of 36) led to weak fluorescence due to strong auto-quenching. In addition, while HDFc12 generated negligible fluorescence due to its small number of conjugations with Ce6, HDFc22 produced an observable red signal, possibly because HDFc22 has the proper amount of Ce6 attachment to discharge the fluorescence by reduced auto-quenching.

The UV–vis-NIR spectrum showed the peak of Ce6 (approximately 670 nm) (Wang et al., 2013) after 1000 U/mL of HAase treatment for 1 h (Fig. 2c). The HD emitted a negligible signal, and HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32 released 2-fold, 3.5-fold, and 4-fold higher fluorescence, respectively than Fc. Moreover, whereas the increase in HAase concentration is not involved in the fluorescence production from HD and Fc, the degree of fluorescence intensity from HDFcs was HAase concentration-dependent. More specifically, HDFc32 presented the highest increase in the fluorescence intensity under 1000 U/mL of HAase application (Fig. S2). The HAase incubation time did not change the fluorescence release from HD and Fc, but the signals from HDFcs were escalated in an HAase treatment time-dependent manner (Fig. S3). Taken together, the UV–vis-NIR data are in line with the qualitative imaging data (Fig. 2a–b).

To calculate the DE of HDFc samples, the fluorescence intensity of each particle under different HAase concentrations was examined (Cuomo, Palazzo, Ceglie, & Lopez, 2009). In this study, the DE of each particle was determined by the ratio of fluorescence intensity of each HDFc to fluorescence intensity of Fc, and it indicates the degree of physicochemical change from auto-quenching to dequenching. For higher DE, Ce6-based fluorescence from HDFc samples should be stronger by the HAase-regulated dequenching mechanism. As a result, HDFc32 showed the highest DE under 1000 U/mL of HAase treatment (Fig. 2d). In particular, both HDFc22 and HDFc32 had a significantly higher DE than Fc of over 120 U/mL, implying that the dequenching state is more dominant than the auto-quenching state from the concentration. However, the HDFc12 did not present a significant increase in DE compared to Fc below 500 U/mL after HAase addition, suggesting that HAase did not remarkably affect the quenching state of HDFc12.

Even though numerous types of fluorescent materials have strong imaging characteristics, they mostly suffer from auto-quenching and the resultant insignificant quantum yield (Wei et al., 2014). To overcome this obstacle and improve quantum yield, we exploited HAase-triggered dequenching of Ce6 in HDFcs. We computed the quantum yield from integrated fluorescence intensity of each HDFc material and compared it with the quantum yield of the Ce6 molecule, 0.16 (Bastien et al., 2015), to validate the enhanced photoluminescence. HDFc22 showed comparable quantum yield to Fc, but both HDFc12 and HDFc32 displayed lower quantum yield than Fc at the baseline (Fig. 2e). As the treated HAase concentration was increased, the quantum yields of HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32 were generally elevated. Particularly, upon 1000 U/mL of HAase application, HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32 exhibited approximately 1.5-fold, 2.4-fold, and 3.5-fold higher quantum yield, respectively, compared to Fc. This is because after dequenching of Ce6 molecules by HAase, the biggest number of coated Ce6 provided HDFc32 with the most effective photoluminescence. Together, the results suggest that the photoactivity of HDFc particles is regulated by the administered concentration of HAase.

To determine if HAase in tumor cells can activate the photoluminescence of HDFcs, and if this feature can be employed to detect tumor cells, we reacted Fc, HDFc12, HDFc22, or HDFc32 with five types of tumor cell lines, including MDA-MB-231, HCT-116, MCF-7, A549, and HeLa cells, and with normal NIH3T3 cells. Interestingly, interplay between the dots and the cells was different, depending on not only the amount of Ce6 molecules conjugated with HDFc but also the cell origin. While MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 showed strong Ce6-based fluorescence, especially from HDFc32 treatment, HCT-117, A549, and HeLa cells discharged a mild signal under exposure to the three HDFcs (Fig. 3 a). It is highly likely that the weaker fluorescence of MCF-7 cells compared to MDA-MB-231 cells is ascribed to lower CD44 expression on MCF-7 cells (Hu et al., 2015; Surace et al., 2009), and this caused less HA-CD44 interaction-mediated endocytosis of HDFc probes. However, NIH3T3 cells released similar photoluminescence after HDFc application to Fc incubation, implying that NIH3T3 cells are not involved in CD44 receptor positivity and/or HAase metabolism (Zhao et al., 2015). Quantitatively, MDA-MB-231, MCF-7, HCT-116, A549, and HeLa cells presented 12-fold, 10-fold, 6-fold, 7-fold, and 5-fold higher fluorescence intensity, respectively, than NIH3T3 cells under HDFc32 administration (Fig. 3b). The different endogenous HAase concentration in each tumor cell line can be responsible for the different HAase-induced dequenching and the consequent fluorescence release of Ce6 in spite of CD44 overexpression on the tumor cells (Dufaÿ Wojcicki et al., 2012; Lee, Park, Noh et al., 2018; Lee, Park, Oh et al., 2018).

Protein expression analysis demonstrated that Hyal-1 expression is cell-type dependent (Fig. 3c). By comparing the endogenous marker of β-actin, Hyal-1 existence in MDA-MB-231, MCF-7, HCT-116, A549, HeLa, and NIH3T3 cells was examined. NIH3T3 cells displayed negligible Hyal-1 expression among them. MDA-MB-231, MCF-7, HCT-116, A549, and HeLa cells had approximately 5.5-fold, 4.8-fold, 4.1-fold, 4-fold, and 3-fold stronger Hyal-1 expression, respectively, which is in line with the qualitative and quantitative Ce6-produced fluorescence results (Fig. 3a–b). For further experiments, MDA-MB-231 cells containing the most Hyal-1, HCT-116 cells possessing a moderate amount of Hyal-1, and NIH3T3 cells including the least amount of Hyal-1 were selected and investigated.

Cellular internalization and intracellular behavior were assessed using confocal microscopy. Fc and HDFcs in MDA-MB-231 cells were monitored to observe their intracellular localization. The HDFc32 exhibited the strongest red fluorescence generated by Ce6 (Fig. 3d). Flow cytometry confirmed the confocal images by showing that HDFc32 was internalized into MDA-MB-231 cells the most (Fig. 3e). Additionally, the cellular uptake of HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32 was consistently increased in a material incubation time-dependent manner. Similarly, the HDFc32 generated the strongest red signal among the Fc-associated particles in HCT-116 cells, and after 8 h administration, the particles were internalized into HCT-116 cells the most (Fig. S4a–b). Importantly, the photoactivity of Ce6 in HCT-116 cells was lower than that of Ce6 in MDA-MB-231 cells, because MDA-MB-231 cells have more enriched HAase than HCT-116 cells (Fig. 3c), and this characteristic more actively frees the HDFcs to create the fluorescence in MDA-MB-231 cells. However, the Ce6-emitted fluorescence (Fig. 3f) and cellular entry (Fig. 3g) into NIH3T3 cells were invisible and negligible, respectively, regardless of the material type. This is mainly due to the CD44 receptor negativity of NIH3T3 cells, leading to ineffective cellular internalization of HA-anchored imaging tools. In addition, although the HDFcs were endocytosed, the insufficient level of Hyal-1 was unable to dequench Ce6 molecules in the particles.

The biomedical interest in imaging cells is centered on the intracellular milieu in living cells and this has been a difficult issue in the field of cell biology. To explore the applications of HDFcs in advanced bioimaging, we tested them in living cells. Whereas Ce6 was not clearly illustrated in Fc-treated MDA-MB-231 cells, the red fluorescence was visualized noticeably in HDFc32- and HDFc22-incubated MDA-MB-231 cells, especially at 60 min post-application (Fig. 4 a). For HDFc12, after 30 min treatment, the strongest signal was observed. The fluorescence imaging validated that the endocytosed HDFcs were gradually disintegrated by HAase in MDA-MB-231 cells as the treatment time was increased, resulting in dequenched Ce6 along with excited photoluminescence. Consistent with the images, HDFc32 produced an 11-fold stronger red signal of Ce6 than Fc in the 60 min incubation period when the red fluorescence was adjusted to cell membrane stained by WGA-Alexa® 488 (Fig. 4b). Of note, after 20 min treatment, the signal by HDFc22 was stronger than that of HDFc32, because the auto-quenching is predominant in HDFc32 at the early phase of HAase addition (Fig. 2b). Additionally, the fluorescence intensity was slightly reduced after 120 min incubation of HDFcs, largely due to the fluorescence loss by continuous imaging. In contrast to the data in MDA-MB-231 cells, Fc, HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32 showed negligible Ce6-released fluorescence in NIH3T3 cells during the entire treatment time (Fig. 4c–d), suggesting that the deficiency of receptor-mediated endocytosis and insignificant Hyal-1 protein expression (Fig. 3c) resulted in the inefficient cellular entry of HDFcs and the maintenance of auto-quenched Ce6 molecules, respectively. Moreover, similar to the imaging of fixed cells, the HDFc32 generated the strongest red signal in HCT-116 cells, but it was lower than the fluorescence produced by the HDFc32 in MDA-MB-231 cells (Fig. S5a–b). For the biocompatibility test, cell integrity after administration of HDFc12, HDFc22, and HDFc32 was determined in MDA-MB-231 (Fig. S6a), HCT-116 (Fig. S6b), and NIH3T3 cells (Fig. S6c) in order to verify their future biomedical application. All of the HDFcs were non-toxic to the cells because of the polysaccharide conjugate HA-originated biocompatibility (Choi et al., 2018; Yuan et al., 2017). In addition, unlike the tumor-detecting fluorescent nanoparticles developed by other research groups (Kelkar, Hill, Marini, & Mohs, 2016; Liu et al., 2015), our hyaluronate dots are ultra-fine particles (size: 3–5 nm, Fig. 1c) that can be easily fabricated, providing tumor diagnosis-promising features due to their well-dispersed property in aqueous solution without extra sonication and their efficient cellular interaction/uptake.

A type of polysaccharidic dot, HDFc, was prepared, characterized, and examined as a tumor cell-specific biomarker. in vitro, the photoluminescence of HDFc imaging agents was cell type-dependent because of the differences in the amounts of CD44 receptors on the cell surface and the degree of endogenous HAase expression in cells. As a result, the Ce6-based fluorescence in the HDFc is off in healthy cells because of its inactivated auto-quenching, and the signal is turned on in abnormal tumor cells caused by dequenching of Ce6 molecules, relying on the tumor cell-specialized endogenous HAase enzyme. Based on the results, it is anticipated that the HDFc imaging agent will allow us to selectively target tumor cells for medical diagnosis in drug delivery systems.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (grant number: NRF-2018R1A2B6000970).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2019.01.049.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
